summary: I grew up in Harlan County, Kentucky, a place deeply rooted in coal mining communities. It was a tight-knit area where towns like Lynch and Benham were closely connected, and the coal camps shaped much of our daily lives. My interview took place here in the Tri-Cities area of Harlan County, where the history of coal miners and their families is still very much alive.

I was born in Lynch, Kentucky, in 1949, and it was home to the U.S. Steel Mine coal camp where my family lived. Lynch had its own independent school system, including the West Main School, which was formerly known as Lynch Colored School. Growing up in Lynch meant being part of a close community where we played with children of all races during the day but respected boundaries at night.

Benham was another coal camp town in Harlan County, home to the International Harvester Mine. Although I was from Lynch, I knew Benham well since it was right next door and the communities were similar. My interviewer, who grew up in Benham, shared memories of the youth center there, which was like our pool room in Lynch.

entity: University of Kentucky Louie B. Nunn Center for Oral History
summary: This interview was made possible by the University of Kentucky Louie B. Nunn Center for Oral History. They generously provided the recording equipment and will store the interview for unrestricted use. It feels important to have my story preserved there so that the history of coal miners’ families, especially women, is not forgotten.

I was born in Lynch at the Notre Dame Hospital, which was the name of the hospital at that time. Since then, the hospital has changed names, now known as Meridzo Center or Solomon's Porch. It was the place where many in our community came into the world.

The hospital where I was born in Lynch was originally called Notre Dame Hospital but is now known as the Meridzo Center. It’s the same place, just under a new name, reflecting changes in the community over the years. This center remains a landmark in Lynch.

Solomon's Porch is another name for the hospital in Lynch where I was born, originally called Notre Dame Hospital. Over time, the facility transitioned to being called Solomon's Porch or Meridzo Center. It’s part of the history of our town’s healthcare services.

Cumberland was the trade area near Lynch and Benham, and later my family moved there after my father retired from mining. Because the company houses were tied to the mines, we had to relocate to Cumberland when we left Lynch. Cumberland had the integrated schools where my siblings finished their education.

International Harvester operated the mine in Benham, Kentucky, one of the coal camps in Harlan County. While I lived in Lynch near the U.S. Steel Mine, I was aware that International Harvester ran a similar mining operation just next door. The two companies’ camps were closely linked in community life.

U.S. Steel operated the coal mine in Lynch, where my family lived in Camp Number Three. My father was a retired coal miner for U.S. Steel, and both my brother and husband worked in the mines there as well. The company’s presence shaped much of our daily life and community structure.

The United Mine Workers of America, or UMWA, played a role in our community, especially during integration. My sister was even chosen as a candidate or queen by the UMWA, which was a proud moment for our family. The union was an important part of coal miners' lives and community events.

My brother enlisted in the United States Army after finishing high school. He served before eventually returning to work in the coal mines in Lynch, following in the footsteps of our family. His time in the Army was a significant chapter in his life before mining.

Gap Branch was one of the mining areas near Lynch where my brother and husband worked. They were assigned to Portal Thirty-five at Gap Branch, which was one of the numbered sections of the mine. It was common to refer to different parts of the mine by their portal numbers.

I attended West Main School in Lynch from first through ninth grade. It was formerly called Lynch Colored School before integration around 1964 or 1965. West Main served as the Black school in the Lynch Independent School District during segregation.

Before it was called West Main, the school I attended was known as Lynch Colored School. It was the segregated school for Black children in Lynch before integration in the mid-1960s. My siblings and I experienced the transition from segregation to integrated schooling there.

Lynch had its own independent school district, separate from nearby towns like Cumberland. This district operated schools like West Main, where my siblings and I went. It was part of the segregated system before integration, and my sister was even the first Black queen in the integrated school.

On Sundays, after church and Sunday school, we often attended the Baptist Training Union in Lynch. It kept us busy in the evenings with activities and fellowship. Attending church and these programs was important, and if we didn’t go, we weren’t allowed to participate in social events like the pool room.

After my family moved to Cumberland following my father’s retirement, my siblings finished their schooling there. Cumberland High School was part of the integrated school system where my sister and brother completed their education. This move marked a shift from the segregated schools of Lynch to integrated ones.

Marion, Indiana, was not mentioned in my story or experiences. My life and memories are centered around Harlan County, Kentucky, and the coal mining communities there. So I don’t have any personal connection to Marion, Indiana.
